title: Computer and Information Research Scientists
keyDrivers: These scientists work at the frontier of computing, tackling genuinely novel problems where pattern-matching from existing solutions fails, the kind of deep, creative reasoning that AI struggles to replicate. They are also the people most likely to be designing, directing, and evaluating AI systems themselves, making their expertise more valuable as AI expands. However, the role lacks physical or legal protections, meaning its resilience depends entirely on staying ahead of the technology curve.

taskData.full: Analyze problems to develop solutions involving computer hardware and software.
Conduct logical analyses of business, scientific, engineering, and other technical problems, formulating mathematical models of problems for solution by computers.
Assign or schedule tasks to meet work priorities and goals.
Apply theoretical expertise and innovation to create or apply new technology, such as adapting principles for applying computers to new uses.
Consult with users, management, vendors, and technicians to determine computing needs and system requirements.
Design computers and the software that runs them.
Develop and interpret organizational goals, policies, and procedures.
Meet with managers, vendors, and others to solicit cooperation and resolve problems.
Maintain network hardware and software, direct network security measures, and monitor networks to ensure availability to system users.
Participate in multidisciplinary projects in areas such as virtual reality, human-computer interaction, or robotics.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: AI coding assistants and automated data pipelines are absorbing the literature review, data processing, and boilerplate model evaluation work that junior researchers spend most of their time on — compressing the entry-level pipeline into the field [1]. Tech industry research lab headcount has contracted sharply since 2022, with major AI labs consolidating teams and shifting from basic research toward applied product work, narrowing the number of academic-style research positions available in industry [2].
risks.statLabel: tech research and engineering roles cut at major AI labs in 2023–2024

opportunities.body: Original hypothesis generation, experimental design, and peer-reviewed publication require scientific judgment that remains structurally resistant to automation — AI accelerates the execution layer but cannot replace the researcher directing it [1]. The volume of AI research published has grown dramatically, creating sustained demand for scientists who can evaluate, critique, and extend the frontier: reviewers, collaborators, and principal investigators who can direct AI-assisted research programs at scale [2].
opportunities.statLabel: increase in AI-related research papers published since 2020

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The research work most protected from displacement is the part that requires genuine originality - identifying novel problems, designing experiments that test real unknowns, and interpreting ambiguous results in context of the field. Start using AI tools to accelerate the literature review and initial modeling phases, which frees more time for the creative and judgment-intensive work at the frontier [1]. Over the next year, build depth in AI research itself or in domain-specific applications of machine learning, since those intersections carry both higher demand and stronger protections than generalist computer science research.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Doctoral degrees are the standard credential for this field, and most positions are in universities, government labs, and the research divisions of large technology companies [4]. The path is long but the role's structural protections are strong - original research output is among the hardest things for AI to generate reliably, and the judgment required to identify what is worth investigating is a persistent human advantage. Build a research portfolio that shows systems-level thinking and the ability to publish findings, not just implement existing methods.

quotes.quote: The research tasks where AI earns its keep are computation, literature synthesis, and pattern detection. What it can't do is identify which question is worth asking, or exercise the scientific skepticism to know when a result is too clean. That judgment is still entirely human — and it's the most professionally valuable part of the job.
quotes.attribution: Communications of the ACM, 'AI as a Research Collaborator'

quotes.quote: The researchers I know who are using AI tools most effectively are treating them like powerful but unreliable collaborators. You have to maintain enough command of the domain to know when the output is wrong — and the people who've let those muscles atrophy are the ones getting into trouble.
quotes.attribution: MIT Sloan Management Review, 'Human Oversight in AI-Assisted Research'

quotes.quote: Private sector research roles at AI labs and cloud platforms now account for a significant share of PhD-level hiring in CS. The distinguishing factor is not just technical depth but the ability to identify tractable research problems within applied constraints. Academic training develops the former — the latter often has to be built on the job.
quotes.attribution: Communications of the ACM, 'Career Pathways in AI Research'

quotes.quote: Publication record matters — but what hiring managers at research labs are increasingly screening for is reproducibility and methodological rigor, not just novel results. The candidates who stand out show their work: open code, documented experiments, and honest ablation studies.
quotes.attribution: MIT Sloan Management Review, 'What Research Labs Look For in New Hires'

careerCluster.title: Information Systems Manager
careerCluster.fit: You already solve complex problems, coordinate with stakeholders, and assign tasks, but this move is a step up into full ownership of budgets, staff, and department operations rather than research outputs.
steps: Earn PMP or CISSP certification
Take on team lead or project manager role
Shadow a CIS manager for 3-6 months

careerCluster.title: Computer Network Architect
careerCluster.fit: Your background in analyzing complex systems and designing technical solutions carries over directly, but the job shifts from research and innovation to hands-on network operations, maintenance, and day-to-day infrastructure management.
steps: Earn Cisco CCNA or CompTIA Network+ certification
Build a home lab running real network configurations
Study firewall tools: pfSense, Cisco ASA, or Palo Alto

careerCluster.title: Data Scientist
careerCluster.fit: A computer and information research scientist moving to data science is a step down in credential and abstraction — the mathematical and statistical foundations carry over entirely, but the job shifts from advancing theory and designing systems to applying existing methods to business data.
steps: No new credential required
Build applied ML/data portfolio in Python (pandas, scikit-learn)
Get familiar with business data tooling: SQL, Tableau, or dbt

careerCluster.title: Information Technology Project Manager
careerCluster.fit: A computer and information research scientist moving to IT project management is a step down in technical depth — your systems and problem-solving background helps you communicate with engineers, but the job shifts from doing technical work to coordinating it.
steps: Earn PMP or PMI-ACP certification
No programming work required in role
Build experience managing cross-functional technical teams

careerCluster.title: Computer Systems Architect
careerCluster.fit: A computer and information research scientist moving to systems engineering is a step down from research to applied work — your deep technical foundations carry over, but the job shifts from advancing knowledge to designing and operating production systems.
steps: No new credential required
Earn AWS or Azure Solutions Architect certification
Build hands-on systems deployment portfolio

emergingCareers.title: AI Safety Researcher
emergingCareers.description: Studies failure modes, alignment properties, and societal risks of large AI systems. Designs and runs experiments on model behavior under distribution shift, adversarial prompting, and reward misspecification. Publishes findings that inform how labs build safer, more predictable AI systems.

stat.text: AI safety roles at frontier labs grew over 200% year-over-year in 2024, driven by regulatory pressure and internal safety commitments — with Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Meta all expanding dedicated safety teams.
stat.sourceTitle: Future of Work Report: AI at Work

emergingCareers.fit: Your research methodology and comfort with formal models of computation transfer directly — alignment work is applied math and empirical science, and your background in CS theory gives you stronger foundations than most safety candidates.
steps: Complete ARENA (Alignment Research Engineer Accelerator) or BlueDot Impact's AI Safety Fundamentals course
Replicate one paper from the mechanistic interpretability literature (e.g. Anthropic's circuit findings) and publish your replication notes publicly
Apply to an AI safety research fellowship: MATS, SPAR, or Anthropic's residency program

emergingCareers.title: ML Research Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Bridges the gap between research and production by implementing, reproducing, and scaling machine learning experiments. Owns the engineering infrastructure that lets research scientists iterate faster — training pipelines, experiment tracking, compute orchestration, and model evaluation frameworks.

stat.text: ML Engineer was among the top 5 fastest-growing job titles on LinkedIn in 2024, with a 40% year-over-year increase in postings and median compensation exceeding $180,000 at frontier AI companies.

emergingCareers.search_query: "ML research engineer" OR "machine learning engineer" research jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your ability to read papers and understand algorithms at a deep level is the hard part — the engineering skills (GPU programming, distributed training) are learnable, and most research labs will train you on infrastructure if you can engage with the math.
steps: Reproduce a recent NeurIPS or ICML paper end-to-end using PyTorch, documenting every discrepancy from the paper's reported results
Complete fast.ai's Practical Deep Learning for Coders and Jeremy Howard's course on training from scratch to build hands-on intuition
Set up a personal Weights & Biases project tracking 10+ experiment runs to demonstrate you understand the research engineering workflow
